paper_title: DeepRefine: Agent-Compiled Knowledge Refinement via Reinforcement Learning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Agent-compiled knowledge bases provide persistent external knowledge for large language model (LLM) agents in open-ended, knowledge-intensive downstream tasks. Yet their quality is systematically limited by \emph{incompleteness}, \emph{incorrectness}, and \emph{redundancy}, manifested as missing evidence or cross-document links, low-confidence or imprecise claims, and ambiguous or coreference resolution issues. Such defects compound under iterative use, degrading retrieval fidelity and downstream task performance. We present \textbf{DeepRefine}, a general LLM-based reasoning model for \emph{agent-compiled knowledge refinement} that improves the quality of any pre-constructed knowledge bases with user queries to make it more suitable for the downstream tasks. DeepRefine performs multi-turn interactions with the knowledge base and conducts abductive diagnosis over interaction history, localizes likely defects, and executes targeted refinement actions for incremental knowledge base updates. To optimize refinement policies of DeepRefine without gold references, we introduce a Gain-Beyond-Draft (GBD) reward and train the reasoning process end-to-end via reinforcement learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent downstream gains over strong baselines.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Agent-compiled knowledge bases provide persistent external knowledge for large language model (LLM) agents in open-ended, knowledge-intensive downstream tasks. Yet their quality is systematically limited by incompleteness, incorrectness, and redundancy, manifested as missing evidence or cross-document links, low-confidence or imprecise claims, and ambiguous or coreference resolution issues. Such defects compound under iterative use, degrading retrieval fidelity and downstream task performance. We present DeepRefine, general LLM-based reasoning model for agent-compiled knowledge refinement that improves the quality of any pre-constructed knowledge bases with user queries to make it more suitable for the downstream tasks. DeepRefine performs multi-turn interactions with the knowledge base and conducts abductive diagnosis over interaction history, localizes likely defects, and executes targeted refinement actions for incremental knowledge base updates. To optimize refinement policies of DeepRefine without gold references, we introduce Gain-Beyond-Draft (GBD) reward and train the reasoning process end-to-end via reinforcement learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent downstream gains over strong baselines.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in handling natural language understanding and generation tasks [Yang et al., 2025, Jaech et al., 2024]. And retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) grounds LLMs in external knowledge bases outside model parameters [Lewis et al., 2020, Gao et al., 2023]. dominant recipe indexes external knowledge as vectors for dense retrieval. Graph-based RAG systems additionally organize salient content into graph-structured knowledge bases to support multi-hop retrieval and reasoning [Edge et al., 2024, Guo et al., 2024, Gutiérrez et al., 2025, Huang et al., 2025, Yang et al., 2026]. In both cases, the external knowledge base is usually built once and static at serving time, which provides LLMs with richer external knowledge and enhances their capabilities in domain-specific or knowledge-intensive tasks. Yet static knowledge base is largely stateless with respect to ongoing use. It does not accumulate structured lessons from repeated queries, user-specific context, or iterative agent workflows. Recent agent-compiled knowledge bases (e.g., LLM-Wiki [Karpathy, 2026]) address this gap by having LLM-based agents ingest sources, synthesize articles, and maintain cross-references over time. For example, toolkits that compile documents using agentic workflows and file outputs back into user-owned persistent store. As shown in the left part of Figure 1, such systems make external knowledge bases evolvable rather than frozen at test time. Contact: hhuangcp@connect.ust.hk Corresponding Author, baijiaxin@comp.hkbu.edu.hk DeepRefine Figure 1: The quality problems of agent-compiled knowledge bases (left) and the challenges of knowledge base refinement (right). Despite this progress, agent-compiled knowledge bases often exhibit systematic quality defects, including incompleteness, incorrectness, and redundancy [Subagdja et al., 2024, Cimiano and Paulheim, 2017]. In practice, these defects manifest as missing evidence chains or cross-document links, low-confidence or imprecise assertions, and ambiguous or coreference resolution issues. Such issues compound and degrade both retrieval fidelity and end-task performance. Recent studies mainly optimize the construction policy of knowledge base builders [Wang et al., 2025, Tsang et al., 2025], however, in many real deployments the knowledge base has already been constructed, so re-training the constructor and re-compiling the whole knowledge base is often expensive and operationally impractical at scale [Choubey et al., 2024, Wolff and Bennati, 2026], motivating post-construction agent-compiled knowledge refinement paradigm. Achieving effective agent-compiled knowledge refinement presents two core challenges: (1) defect localization, i.e., identifying problematic regions in large knowledge base without performing expensive traversal checks; and (2) policy optimization without golden references, i.e., learning effective refinement strategies when no reference refinement actions or golden knowledge bases are available. As shown in the right part of Figure 1, Challenge (1) arises because compiled knowledge bases are typically large and relationally connected, making exhaustive inspection infeasible for locating defects that stem from incompleteness, incorrectness, or redundancy [Dong et al., 2025]. Challenge (2) arises because the LLM-based agents have difficulty refining knowledge bases and learning how to refine knowledge bases. The real-world refinement lacks golden references. We generally do not observe the correct sequence of refinement actions that best aligns knowledge base with downstream tasks. Consequently, learning effective policies requires training objectives that can evaluate refinement quality through downstream task utility signals. To address these challenges, we propose DeepRefine, general LLM-based reasoning model for agent-compiled knowledge refinement that improves any pre-constructed knowledge base with user queries to make it more suitable for the downstream tasks. DeepRefine follows three-step reasoning process: Answerability Judgement Loop, Error Abduction, and Refinement Actions Generation. For Challenge (1), instead of traversing the full knowledge base, DeepRefine performs multi-turn query-conditioned interaction with external knowledge to localize potentially defective neighborhoods, substantially reducing refinement complexity while preserving effectiveness. For Challenge (2), DeepRefine abductively infers likely defect causes from interaction histories, then generates refinement actions to update the knowledge base incrementally. Because the utility of refinement actions is only observable through downstream task performance and is non-differentiable, we introduce Gain-Beyond-Draft (GBD) reward and optimize the refinement policy end-to-end via reinforcement learning. In summary, we make the following main contributions: To the best of our knowledge, DeepRefine is the first general LLM-based reasoning model for agent-compiled knowledge refinement, capable of refining any pre-constructed knowledge base using only user queries to make it more suitable for the downstream tasks. We design three-step reasoning process including Answerability Judgement Loop, Error Abduction, and Refinement Actions Generation. We further introduce Gain-BeyondDraft (GBD) reward with reinforcement learning to enhance the knowledge base refinement capabilities of LLMs. 2 We conduct extensive experiments across five datasets to demonstrate that DeepRefine can steadily improve the performance of state-of-the-art baseline methods.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: 2.1 Agent-Compiled Knowledge Base Management Agent-compiled knowledge bases are effective because they usually capture relational and multi-hop dependencies, organize evidence hierarchically [Huang et al., 2025, Zhang et al., 2025], and support efficient retrieval [Yang et al., 2026]. Different from graph-enhanced RAG methods that largely treat constructed structures as static artifacts [Edge et al., 2024, Jimenez Gutierrez et al., 2024], recent agent memory systems (e.g., Zep [Rasmussen et al., 2025], Mem0 [Chhikara et al., 2025]) emphasize continuous maintenance and evolution. Existing RL-based approaches, such as Mem-α [Wang et al., 2025], Memory-R1 [Yan et al., 2025], and AutoGraph-R1 [Tsang et al., 2025], primarily optimize construction-time knowledge base management policies. In contrast, our work focuses on post-construction refinement of already deployed agent-compiled knowledge bases. 2.2 LLMs and Reinforcement Learning Reinforcement learning (RL) [Kaelbling et al., 1996] is an effective optimization framework for improving the sequential decision-making capabilities of LLMs through learning from environmental interaction and reward feedback [Xi et al., 2025]. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) [Ouyang et al., 2022] is one of the foundational methods used to align LLM outputs with preferences. And Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) [Schulman et al., 2017], and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [Shao et al., 2024] have provided scalable and efficient optimization algorithms for various structured decision-making tasks in several domains. For example, GraphR1 [Luo et al., 2025] employs RL to learn effective graph-structured tools navigation to retrieve more accurately. Search-R1 [Jin et al., 2025] trains LLMs to optimize web search queries using RL to maximize final answer correctness and s3 [Jiang et al., 2025] further proposes principled, model-agnostic reward signal that quantifies improvements over standard retrieval. 2.3 Knowledge Refinement Knowledge refinement aims to mitigate incompleteness, incorrectness, and redundancy in existing artifacts [Subagdja et al., 2024, Cimiano and Paulheim, 2017]. Classical knowledge graph (KG) completion is one of the knowledge refinement methods, such as KG-BERT [Yao et al., 2019] and KGRefiner [Saeedizade et al., 2022], are typically limited to adding links or relations. Entity alignment and disambiguation methods, such as NeuSymEA [Chen et al., 2024] and K-NED [Feng et al., 2020], focus on correction and simplification. In contrast, knowledge refinement has broader scope that subsumes both types of operations [Cimiano and Paulheim, 2017], yet remains underexplored. TRAIL [Zhao et al., 2025] enables LLM-based agents to iteratively explore and update KGs during reasoning, however, its edits are not explicitly optimized against downstream task utility. Our method instead learns refinement policies with an explicit downstream reward, yielding tighter alignment between knowledge base updates and end-task performance.

sections.title: 3 Preliminaries
sections.content: In this section, we formalize the knowledge base and the refinement task with respect to RAG-based downstream tasks. We represent knowledge base as structured triples that are more general and not limited to classical KG triples. The heads and tails may be entities, events, or document-grounded spans, which we term as knowledge items, and relations may capture taxonomy, time, causality, or cross-document linkage. We denote the pre-constructed knowledge base by set of triples Gf = {(h, r, t) h, Ef, Rf}, where Ef is the set of knowledge items and Rf is the set of relations. The knowledge base may be produced by any LLM-based constructors. We are given set of user queries = {q1, q2, . . . , qN } related to the Gf. The goal of agent-compiled knowledge base refinement is to update Gf with user queries in to make it more suitable for the downstream RAG tasks. 3 Figure 2: Demonstration of GRPO training with agent-compiled knowledge refinement (DeepRefine), where the orange arrows denote the multi-turn interactions of DeepRefine with knowledge bases and the green arrows denote the refinement actions on knowledge bases. Then we further formulate knowledge base refinement as generating sequences of actions that mitigate incompleteness, incorrectness, and redundancy in Gf. This process can be represented as: arg max SAq pθ(S Gf, Q) = {A1 q, . . . , AQ }, (1) where SAq is the set of action sequence Ai = {a1, a2, . . . , aMi} with respect to the i-th query Q, is the possible output set, and pθ is the policy model parameterized by θ. These actions are then applied to improve the quality of the knowledge base Gf.

sections.title:
4 Methodology
4.1 Reasoning Steps Design
sections.content: Answerable Judgement Loop. Initially, DeepRefine will judge the answerability of the given query with several interactions with the knowledge base Gf, as illustrated in the loop of the rollout module in Figure 2. If the query is answerable in the first interaction, DeepRefine will directly skip that. Otherwise, DeepRefine will refine Gf in later reasoning steps to make it more easily to be answered. In the first interaction with Gf, as shown in Eq. 2, DeepRefine will conduct simple dense retrieval to obtain the top-N related triples to the query with their embeddings, which can be considered as 0-hop retrieved subgraph G(0) . G(0) = Top-k(q, Gf, ). (2) Then for each of the following interactions, DeepRefine will continue to expand the retrieved subgraph if the answerability is not satisfied in the previous interaction. To be specific, the subgraph expanding procedure is defined as follows: G(i) cand = {(h, r, t) Gf or (i1) }, pruned = Top-k(q, G(i) G(i) G(i) = G(i1) G(i) pruned, cand, ), (3) (4) (5) is the knowledge item set of the subgraph G(i1) where G(i) cand is set of new candidate triples that connect to the knowledge items in the subgraph G(i1) and (i1) . To control the size of the retrieved subgraph with respect to the query q, we further prune the candidate triples with their embeddings, and select the top-M related triples to the query, as shown in Eq. 4. Then we merge these new triples with the previous subgraph and obtain the i-hop retrieved subgraph G(i) . The process of interactions will continue until the answerability is satisfied or the maximum number of expansion hops is reached. The judgement output will be enclosed within <judge> and </judge> tags as the prompt template demonstrated in Figure 6 of Appendix E. After it terminates, the query-specific interaction history Hq with total length of can be finally obtained for the following reasoning steps. The interaction history consists of the retrieved subgraph at each interaction step and the query: Hq = L1 (cid:91) i=0 {(q, G(i) , (i) )}, (6) 4 where (i) is the answerable judgement result of the query at the i-th interaction step. Due to context length limitations of the LLMs, we set horizon length Lh for the interaction history. Thus, the last Lh interaction experiences Hq[Lh :] will be used for the subsequent reasoning steps. Error Abduction. We assume that there are some potential issues in the knowledge triples of query-specific subgraph if the query is not answerable with the 0-hop retrieved subgraph. In this reasoning step, given the interaction history Hq[Lh :], DeepRefine will abductively reason the potential issues Iq in the retrieved subgraphs from three perspectives: incompleteness, errors and redundancy, which are based on the definition of KG refinement [Subagdja et al., 2024] proposed by Cimiano and Paulheim [2017]. The error reasons will be generated within the <abduction> and </abduction> tags as the prompt template demonstrated in Figure 7 of Appendix E. For example, if the query is about multi-hop relationship between two knowledge items, but the retrieved subgraphs wrongly include or even do not contain some intermediate knowledge items or relations, DeepRefine will reason the incorrect or missing parts that may lead to the unanswerable result. Similarly, as for redundancy, DeepRefine will also check if there is any ambiguous knowledge that would affect the answerability. The analysed results in this step will be served as the reference for the following refinement actions generation. Refinement Actions Generation. Based on the analysed potential issues Iq in the subgraphs retrieved from the previous interactions, DeepRefine will edit the full knowledge base Gf accordingly to improve its quality. To reduce token costs and improve the refinement efficiency, instead of reconstructing the subgraphs and then inserting into the full knowledge base, for each query q, DeepRefine will generate series of refinement actions Aq within the <refinement> and </refinement> tags to edit the full knowledge base directly: Aq = arg max AqA pθ(A G(L) , Iq), (7) where is the set of possible output from DeepRefine parameterized by θ. The generated refinement actions will be parsed into three pre-defined types of operators: insert_edge(), delete_edge(), and replace_node(). The action scope is designed to refine the knowledge base along the three main targets discussed above. Edge insertion action complements missing relations or knowledge items to address incompleteness. Edge deletion action removes incorrect or redundant relations to mitigate errors and redundancy. And node replacement action resolves ambiguity to further reduce redundancy. Note that these actions are integrations of common atom operators defined in graph databases like NetworkX [Hagberg and Conway, 2020]. For example, the node replacement action can be implemented through an integration of the original node deletion, original node related edge deletion, new node insertion and the edge insertion of the new node. So the three pre-defined operators are just interfaces, which makes this paradigm can be generalizably applied in any databases. After applying the generated refinement actions, we will obtain the refined knowledge base ˆGf. 4.2 Refinement Policy Optimization Reward Design. To train DeepRefines knowledge refinement policy πθ, we consider knowledge refinement as an end-to-end RL problem. Specifically, as shown in the Figure 2, we introduce Gain Beyond Draft (GBD) as the reward objective, which quantifies the gain in RAG generation accuracy achieved by the refined knowledge base ˆGf relative to the draft knowledge base Gf: GBD(q) = ACC(Arefined, A) ACC(Adraft, A), (8) where Arefined and Adraft are the generated answers with the refined knowledge base ˆGf and the draft one Gf, which are both obtained through the simple dense retrieval method we mentioned before. is the golden answer for the query and ACC(, ) is task-specific metric following the prior work [Jiang et al., 2025] (details see Appendix A.2). Policy Optimization. To optimize the knowledge base refinement policy πθ of DeepRefine, we adopt the Group-Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [Shao et al., 2024] algorithm to finetune the policy with the GBD reward. Similar to Tsang et al. [2025], we simplify the training procedure by removing the KL divergence term in the loss computation and considering that as the reward penalty to lower the computational overhead and save memory usage without damaging the training [Liu et al., 2025, Hu et al., 2025]. Formally, the objective is defined as: JGRPO(θ) = E[q PQ, {oi} πθold(q)] =

During the training process, DeepRefine will generate the knowledge refinement actions together with the reasoning steps we described in Section 4.1 token by token, where oi = {oi,1, . . . , oi,T } represents the sequence of tokens forming the full reasoning output at the i-th group of samples. And the reward signal Ri for the i-th query sample is calculated through the reward function, as illustrated in Eq. (8). ϵ is small clipping hyperparameter that ensures stable updates by preventing the new policy from straying too far from the old policy. σR 4.3 Inference After the RL training, DeepRefine can be directly plugged into any pre-constructed knowledge base. As shown in Figure 3, during inference time, there could be two streams: one is the online query stream, the other one is the knowledge base evolve stream. The first stream is intuitive, because users will sequentially ask the agent-compiled knowledge base questions once it has been constructed. And the knowledge refinement process conducted by frozen DeepRefine with respect to an online query will forma the second stream. Note that the refinement process can be asynchronous with the online service, which will not introduce any systematic latency. DeepRefine will process these queries sequentially with multi-step reasoning chains to improve the quality of the agent-compiled knowledge base and make it more suitable for the downstream tasks. Figure 3: An overview of the inference process of DeepRefine.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: In this section, we will report the performance of DeepRefine on both RAG and long-term conversation memory benchmarks in Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) settings. We focus on the performance gain of DeepRefine over various baseline methods and the efficiency of the knowledge refinement process. 5.1 Experimental Settings Implementation Details. We implement the DeepRefine training and inference processes using both of the Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507en as the backbone model for their better instruction following capabilities. And we employ the Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B as the embedding model for all vector search in both our method and the baseline methods and the frozen Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the reader model for the RAG generation. For the 4B model, we use batch size of 32 with mini-batch size of 16 and group size of 6 for the 60 steps GRPO training. The learning rate is 5e-7. For the 8B model, we use batch size of 64 with mini-batch size of 16 and group size of 6 for the 30 steps GRPO training. The learning rate is 1e-7. To further improve the efficiency of the refinement process, we select subset of queries before refinement using greedy maximum-coverage procedure over query-related triples, as described in Appendix B. Our models are trained on 8 NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB GPUs. Baselines. We select baseline methods from two perspectives. On the one hand, we evaluate the performance gain from DeepRefine over various knowledge base constructor: base knowledge base constructor, which is denoted as Base, the RL fine-tuned constructor, which employs AutoGraphR1 [Tsang et al., 2025] and is denoted as AR1, and the agent-compiled knowledge constructor, which is version of LLM-Wiki and employs Graphify [Shamsi, 2026]. Both Base and AR1 share the 6 same base model Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct. And we uses the Composer-1.5 of Cursor as the agent in Graphify. On the other hand, conditioned on each of the above knowledge base constructor, we evaluate whether the performance gains brought by DeepRefine remain stable across various RAG methods. For graph knowledge retrieval, we employ Subgraph Retriever and ToG. For graph-based text retrieval, we employ HippoRAG [Jimenez Gutierrez et al., 2024] and HippoRAG2 [Gutiérrez et al., 2025]. And we use the same settings of them as those in AutoGraph-R1. Training Datasets. We randomly select 5, 000 samples from the training set of HotpotQA [Yang et al., 2018] to construct the training data for DeepRefine, and split them into training and validation sets with ratio of 8:2. For each original HotpotQA data point, we construct an individual knowledge base. During the rollout process of the training, DeepRefine only refines the full knowledge base associated with the query in each sample individually. Evaluation Datasets. We evaluate DeepRefine on three types of OOD benchmarks, which include Simple QA, Multi-hop QA and Conversation QA. For simple QA, we use the Natural Questions (NQ) [Adelani et al., 2021] and PopQA [Mallen et al., 2023] datasets. For multi-hop QA, we use the 2WikiMultihopQA [Ho et al., 2020] and Musique [Trivedi et al., 2022] datasets. For conversation QA, we use the LOCOMO [Maharana et al., 2024] dataset. For NQ and PopQA, we adopt unified knowledge corpus constructed from the lead sections of the December 2021 Wikipedia dump [Izacard et al., 2023]. For 2WikiMultihopQA and Musique, we construct benchmark-specific corpora from the documents associated with each datasets 1, 000 evaluation instances, consistent with the settings of Jimenez Gutierrez et al. [2024]. Similarly, for LOCOMO, we also sample 1, 000 evaluation instances from the dataset. 5.2 Experimental Results DeepRefine brings consistent downstream gains over various knowledge base constructors and knowledge retrievers. We refine the knowledge bases constructed by the naive constructor (Naive), the RL fine-tuned constructor (AR1) and the agent-compiled knowledge constructor (Graphify). And we evaluate the performance of the original knowledge bases and the refined knowledge bases with various knowledge retrievers under the OOD settings. As shown in Table 1, with DeepRefine-8B, the quality of the constructed knowledge bases can be stably improved for their better downstream performance in most cases. Although there are some cases where DeepRefine degrades the performance, the drops are marginal and slight compared with the cases where DeepRefine improves the performance. This is mainly because with multiple interactions with the external knowledge base, DeepRefine can abduct the potential issues in the knowledge base and then generate refinement actions to fix them. And DeepRefine-8B can perform better than DeepRefine-4B, which suggests the effectiveness of the knowledge refinement process can be improved by larger scale model. Note that our evaluations are fully under the OOD settings, which demonstrates the generalization ability of DeepRefine. DeepRefine achieves this by being optimized with the GBD reward finetuned RL policy, which is generalizable reward function for the knowledge refinement task. We have also demonstrate some cases about how DeepRefine can resolve issues in the constructed knowledge bases in Appendix D. DeepRefine can sometimes introduce larger or competitive downstream gains than the reconstruction method. The downstream performance of the knowledge bases constructed by naive knowledge constructor can even outperform AR1 in some cases after they are optimized by DeepRefine, even though DeepRefine does not optimize the whole knowledge base content like the reconstruction method AR1 does. For example, in 2WikiQA, the performance of HippoRAG2+DeepRefine with the naive constructor is higher than that of HippoRAG with AR1 (53.02 vs. 52.61). And in Musique, the performance of HippoRAG+DeepRefine with the naive constructor is much higher than that of HippoRAG with AR1 (28.27 vs. 26.21). This is because the downstream performance gain may not always be related to the whole knowledge base content, but may only depend on some specific smaller regions of the knowledge base. Regenerating the whole knowledge base may introduce additional noise that may harm the downstream performance. Knowledge base quality and refinement effectiveness can complement each other. As shown in Table 1, the downstream performance gain introduced by DeepRefine would be higher when the refinement process is done on the knowledge bases constructed by AR1 or LLM-Wiki methods. This is mainly because DeepRefine locates the potentially problematic regions in the knowledge bases by iterative subgraph expanding during the multiple interactions with the knowledge base. And the 7 Table 1: Performance of our GBD reward finetuned DeepRefine with Qwen3-8B on various knowledge retrievers. The table reports RAG F1 on five QA datasets, grouped by three constructors: Naive, AR1, and Graphify (LLM-Wiki). Each block lists four retrievers and their DeepRefine-4B as well as their DeepRefine-8B counterpart. Highlighted cells show whether using DeepRefine improves or degrades the performance compared to the original knowledge bases. Table 2: The average time consumptions (s) of the knowledge refinement process of DeepRefine and the reconstruction process of AutoGraph-R1. Methods Simple QA Multi-hop QA Conversation QA AutoGraph-R1 DeepRefine 6,782.8 3,201. 9,780.4 3,357.5 2,826.5 1,115.8 DeepRefine is more efficient than reconstructing the full knowledge base. We collect the average time consumptions of both the refinement method DeepRefine and the reconstruction method AutoGraph-R1. As shown in Table 2, on all three different kinds of benchmark data, DeepRefine exhibits obvious superiority with respect to the reconstruction method. The efficiency of DeepRefine comes from two aspects. First, instead of taking into account all documents and regenerating the whole knowledge base, the agentic framework of DeepRefine makes the model will only focus on the important and potentially problematic parts, which largely reduces the amount of information that is needed to be processed by the model. Further more, the design of generating refinement actions with the format of code to edit the knowledge base can also further reduce the decoding time cost, which eliminates the need of inefficiently regenerating the subgraph knowledge. Second, as illustrated in Appendix B, we select subset of queries before the refinement process using greedy maximum-coverage procedure over query-related triples, which further improves the efficiency by 8 avoiding duplicated refinement of the same region of the full knowledge base. Consequently, taking into account both efficiency and effectiveness, DeepRefine is better choice to improve the quality of agent-compiled knowledge base than the reconstruction methods. Table 3: Performance comparisons between GBD reward finetuned DeepRefine and the DeepRefine without RL training under AR1 and Graphify (LLM-Wiki) constructors, which is denoted as DeepRefine-8B w/o RL. Highlighted cells show whether using DeepRefine improves or degrades the performance compared to the original knowledge bases. Constructor Methods AR1 Graphify (LLM-Wiki) Subgraph Subgraph + DeepRefine-8B w/o RL Subgraph + DeepRefine-8B ToG ToG + DeepRefine-8B w/o RL ToG + DeepRefine-8B HippoRAG HippoRAG + DeepRefine-8B w/o RL HippoRAG + DeepRefine-8B HippoRAG2 HippoRAG2 + DeepRefine-8B w/o RL HippoRAG2 + DeepRefine-8B Subgraph Subgraph + DeepRefine-8B w/o RL Subgraph + DeepRefine-8B ToG ToG + DeepRefine-8B w/o RL ToG + DeepRefine-8B HippoRAG HippoRAG + DeepRefine-8B w/o RL HippoRAG + DeepRefine-8B HippoRAG2 HippoRAG2 + DeepRefine-8B w/o RL HippoRAG2 + DeepRefine-8B Simple QA NQ* 29.27 29.68 29.41 29.88 29.49 29.91 39.84 39.86 40.09 37.25 38.53 38.60 20.42 20.58 21.26 16.69 17.10 18.22 13.23 11.84 13.46 13.62 12.84 13.77 PopQA* 58.40 58.94 58.99 60.34 60.25 61.20 64.43 64.53 64.63 64.69 65.86 65.24 11.38 15.59 15.78 8.97 10.47 10.61 5.57 5.11 6.42 6.19 5.41 6.69 Multi-hop QA 2WikiQA Musique 35.60 38.29 37.76 48.56 51.71 50.13 50.34 51.91 52.75 52.61 55.12 55.45 25.37 26.09 26.34 21.46 20.74 21.55 29.03 29.68 31.61 27.98 30.83 32.91 14.42 15.17 15.86 19.06 19.52 20.14 26.21 27.83 27.44 27.96 27.75 30.30 11.69 11.76 12.43 11.73 12.01 12.33 8.67 8.38 8.76 8.10 8.09 8.45 Conversation Overall LOCOMO 26.65 26.71 27.79 23.64 25.50 26.16 35.02 34.14 35.64 35.75 35.21 36.75 Avg. 32.87 33.76 33.96 36.30 37.29 37.51 43.17 43.65 44.11 43.65 44.49 45. 5.24 5.92 5.95 7.30 7.60 7.39 3.44 4.53 4.33 3.57 3.68 4.09 14.82 15.99 16.35 13.23 13.58 14.02 11.99 11.91 12.92 11.89 12.17 13.18 5.3 Ablation Study Figure 4: The average GBD reward curves during the end-to-end RL training process of both Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507en (left) and Qwen3-8B (right). In this section, to demonstrate the effectiveness and necessity of RL training for the DeepRefine, we further conduct the refinement process without RL training, which is denoted as DeepRefine-8B w/o RL. And then we compare the performance among the original knowledge bases, and the two variants of the DeepRefine. As shown in Table 3, there are marginal improvements or even more worse performance with the naive DeepRefine. This is mainly because without RL optimization, it would be hard for the agent to fully utilize the interaction history in the knowledge refinement task. Besides, the refinement policy without optimization would also be not very useful for the downstream tasks. While with RL training, DeepRefine can leverage the interaction history with the knowledge base to refine the knowledge base, which leads to larger downstream performance gain at most cases. We also further construct benchmark and conduct experiments to verify the effectiveness of the RL training for resolving the incompleteness, incorrectness and redundancy issues in Appendix C. Besides, as shown in Figure 4, we also demonstrate the average GBD reward curves during the RL trainings of both Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507en and Qwen3-8B, which suggests that the optimized knowledge refinement policy πθ could lead to larger downstream performance gain as the training progress goes on.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we propose DeepRefine, general LLM-based reasoning model for agent-compiled knowledge refinement. DeepRefine is designed to iteratively refine any pre-constructed knowledge base by leveraging the multiple interactions with the knowledge base, which is trained with newly designed GBD reward finetuned RL policy. Experimental results under the OOD settings show that DeepRefine can consistently improve the quality of the constructed knowledge base and achieve better downstream performance compared with that of the original knowledge base.

sections.title: A Details for Metrics
sections.content: A.1 Metrics for Benchmark Evaluation Following existing works [Trivedi et al., 2022, Jimenez Gutierrez et al., 2024, Yan et al., 2025], we employ the token-level F1 score as the metric for benchmark evaluation. A.2 Metrics for GBD Reward Following the prior work [Jiang et al., 2025], we also use the Generation Accuracy (GenAcc) as the task-specific metric for the GBD reward, which is more robust and stable metric than the token-level F1 score and is more suitable for RL training of DeepRefine. The GenAcc is defined as follows: GenAcc = span_check judge_check, (10) which is combination of span-matching test [Ma et al., 2021, Lin et al., 2021] and LLM-based correctness check with DeepSeek-V3 [Liu et al., 2024] as judge. Additionally, to effectively shape the refinement behavior of DeepRefine with the GBD reward, we further design transition-based reward matrix that evaluates the models action by comparing the GenAcc of the draft answer (Adraft) and the refined answer (Arefined). Specifically, we define the reward gain R(Adraft, Arefined) over four possible GenAcc transition states: successful correction (0 1) receives the highest reward of +1.0 to strongly incentivize the model to leverage retrieved evidence for error correction, which is the same as the original performance gain of the GBD reward; an erroneous alteration of correct draft (1 0) incurs penalty of -0.3 to discourage over-editing, which is bigger than the original performance gain 1.0 in the GBD reward to prevent the policy from receiving excessive penalties when performing exploration; correct preservation (1 1) receives modest reward of +0.2 to reinforce confidence in already accurate answers; and failed correction (0 0) is assigned neutral reward of 0.0, as penalizing this state would disproportionately destabilize training given its high natural frequency in complex multi-hop reasoning tasks. Coverage-Based Query Selection Considering there could be overlappings among the effective regions of the refinement processes with different queries in each benchmark dataset, to make the knowledge refinement process both efficient and effective, we select small subset of queries before refinement using greedy maximum-coverage procedure over query-related triples. For each candidate query respect to the full knowledge base in benchmark dataset, we obtain set of triples by (i) taking the top-k related triples with their sentence embeddings, and (ii) augmenting with up to additional triples drawn from the onehop neighborhoods of the entities appearing in those triples, where neighborhood triples are ranked by embedding similarity to the query Table 4: Hyperparameters for greedy maximum-coverage query selection. Parameter Value ρ 10 100 / 500 1000 1.0 / 0.8 13 and the highest-scoring ones are kept. Treating each distinct triple as coverage element, we greedily choose queries that maximize the number of newly covered elements until either budget of queries is reached or the fraction of covered elements in the union of all candidates sets exceeds target threshold ρ. Only the selected queries are then passed through the refinement pipeline to update the shared knowledge base. Then all benchmark queries are evaluated on the resulting knowledge base with the standard RAG pipeline. The corresponding hyperparameter values are listed in Table 4. For LOCOMO dataset, is set to 100 and ρ is set to 1.0. For other larger datasets, is set to 500 and ρ is set to 0.8.

sections.title: C Additional Experiments
sections.content: To further evaluate the effectiveness of DeepRefine on resolving incompleteness, incorrectness or redundancy issues, we provide rigorous evaluation. We construct high-quality benchmark characterized by explicit error types and diverse corruption patterns. By editing the knowledge bases with controlled defects that mirror real-world failure modes, it enables precise assessment of models performance in defect localization and refinement processes across various error types. We constuct the benchmark based on the HotpotQA (Dev-Distractor) [Yang et al., 2018] dataset. It consists of 501 samples, with 167 samples dedicated to each of the three systematic error types defined in our study. Each data point includes the original query, the corrupted knowledge base, and comprehensive metadata (e.g., corruption details and action counts). The construction pipeline utilizes Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct (denoted as the corruption model) for generating defects, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct as the base knowledge base constructor, and Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B for vector-based retrieval. Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct serves as the reader model for RAG-based evaluation. Error Types and Corruption Strategies. For each error type, we apply specific corruption strategy to high-quality knowledge base: Incompleteness: We identify and remove critical triples that are essential for the reasoning path, creating "missing evidence" scenarios. Incorrectness: We modify correct knowledge items into incorrect claims based on the original context, simulating lowconfidence or imprecise information. Redundancy: This is achieved via two methods: (1) Inserting semantically similar but redundant triples to introduce ambiguity. (2) Replacing entities in existing triples with aliases or synonymous names to create coreference resolution challenges. Construction Pipeline. The construction pipeline follows rigorous multi-stage pipeline. Initially, for each sample, we construct an individual KB based on its context. We then perform RAG. Only samples achieving F1 score higher than 0.95 are retained. This ensures that the initial KB is relatively accurate and contains sufficient information to answer the query. Then, for the filtered samples, the corruption process is specifically targeted at the top-N triples retrieved during the previous RAG stage. This ensures that the induced defects are concentrated on the critical knowledge components most relevant to the query, rather than introducing peripheral noise. Within this focused scope, the corruption model generates series of actions. To ensure the generation of ideal, highquality defects, we designed specialized prompt templates and carefully curated few-shot samples tailored to each specific error type. These actions are subsequently parsed into three predefined operators: insert_edge(), delete_edge(), and replace_node(). The action space is designed to be symmetric with DeepRefines refinement action set to facilitate direct performance mapping. Notably, the replace_node() operator is restricted to acting upon single, specific edge. This fine-grained precision allows us to create nuanced coreference resolution issues and subtle semantic defects that test the models diagnostic depth. To maintain the structural integrity and ensure the refinability of the knowledge base, the total number of corruption actions is strictly limited to 5 per sample. Then we perform second RAG evaluation on the corrupted KB. Only samples where the RAG F1 score drops below 0.6 are included in the final benchmark. This ensures that the corruption is effective, which suggests that it significantly impairs the models ability to answer the query without refinement. 14 Figure 5: The refinement effectiveness of DeepRefine-8B and Qwen3-8B on resolving three types of issues. We evaluate DeepRefine-8B and Qwen3-8B on the constructed benchmark with 2-hop interaction. And here are some observations and analysis based on the experimental results on the constructed benchmark. The agentic framework designed in DeepRefine is effective for resolving incompleteness, incorrectness and redundancy issues. As shown in Figure 5, both DeepRefine-8B and Qwen3-8B that employ the agentic framework can effectively improve the downstream performance of knowledge bases with the incompleteness, incorrectness and redundancy issues in different degrees. Specifically, the incompleteness issue degrades the downstream performance the most and can also be improved the most easily. This is because lacking evidence can be the most intuitive cause that leads to an unanswerable query and adding supporting evidence can be the most effective way to improve the performance. And the incorrectness issue can also influence the downstream performance very hard, and it is harder to be resolved. Because it requires the model to have the capability to identify the incorrect information and then correct it according to the source passages. Besides, the redundancy issue degrades the downstream performance at moderate level, and it is also the hardest to be resolved. Because it requires the model to do the coreference resolution and disambiguation, which is more challenging than the other two tasks. The DeepRefine model that is optimized on the GBD reward is more effective than the base model. As shown in Figure 5, DeepRefine-8B that is optimized on the GBD reward is more effective than the base model Qwen3-8B in resolving the incompleteness, incorrectness and redundancy issues. Specifically, the superiority of DeepRefine-8B over Qwen3-8B is more significant for the redundancy issue, which is the hardest to be resolved as we mentioned before. The base model has almost no effectiveness for fixing the redundancy issue, however, the DeepRefine-8B can improve the performance to certain extent. And DeepRefine-8B also exhibits much superior performance over Qwen3-8B for resolving the incompleteness and incorrectness issues. This is because the GBD reward can provide more accurate and informative feedback for the agentic refinement process during the RL training, which helps the model to learn both effective and general refinement policy for resolving the incompleteness, incorrectness and redundancy issues.

sections.title: D Case Study
sections.content: In this section, we will provide case study to demonstrate the effectiveness of DeepRefine. We will give some cases to show how DeepRefine can refine the knowledge base to deal with the potential incompleteness, incorrectness or redundancy issues. D.1 Case 1: Incompleteness Incompleteness is the most common issue in the knowledge base. It is not possible to connect all potentially relevant knowledge items in the real world, which makes it necessary to do the completions that are useful for the downstream tasks. As shown in Table 5, the question asks for the 2010 population of the city that is popular with tourists. In the original query-related knowledge items, there are tourism-related clues and several population facts, but the key bridge from the target city to its 2010 population is missing. DeepRefine addresses this incompleteness by inserting series of 15 missing population edges (e.g., city population in 2010 value), which shortens the reasoning path and makes the answer directly retrievable. Query What is the population in 2010 of the city popular with tourists? Query-Related Triples ... <Water tourism inside Strasbourg,attracts,hundreds of thousands of tourists yearly>, <Oklahoma,included four cities over 100,000 in population,in 2010>, <New York City,population reached all-time high,in the 2010 Census>, <Nanjing,has annual attraction,thousands of tourists>, <Oklahoma City,had the largest metropolitan area in the state in 2010,with 1,252,987 people> Original Passages ... Instead, Nanjing, as popular tourist destination, hosts series of government-organised events throughout the year... The total population of the City of Nanjing reached 8.005 million in 2010. The statistics in 2011 estimated the total population to be 8.11 million... Camdenton is city in Camden County, Missouri, United States. The population was 3,718 at the 2010 census... ... Refinement Actions mil- <refinement>insert_edge(Palermo, 2010, milpopulation lion)insert_edge(Nanjing, 799)insert_edge(Quincy, population in 2010, lion)insert_edge(Tranquillity, 2010, 7,972)insert_edge(York, in population 910)insert_edge(Oklahoma City, population in 2010, 620,602)insert_edge(New York City, population in 2010, 8.17 million)insert_edge(Seattle, population in 2010, 608,000)insert_edge(Oklahoma, population in 2010, 3.8 million)</refinement> population in 1.2 8.005 population 2010, 2010, in in Table 5: The case of refinement for incompleteness issue. D.2 Case 2: Incorrectness Errors usually come from the incorrect or conflicting information in the original passages. As shown in Table 6, the error in this case is factual contradiction introduced by misalignment between the knowledge base content and the ground truth evidence. Specifically, the retrieved query-related triples contains wrong edge <Kree Harrison, took runner-up spot, American Idol (season 3)> which contradicts the gold evidence stating that Diana DeGarmo was the runner-up of that season. If this incorrect edge is preserved, the model will inevitably reach the wrong answer regarding the contestants identity and subsequent reasoning hops. DeepRefine handles this through its refinement mechanism: it identifies the mismatch and applies delete_edge as well as insert_edge to restore the correct factual link <Diana DeGarmo, runnerup of, American Idol (season 3)> while effectively overriding the incorrect noise. This ensures the reasoning chain remains faithful to the supporting paragraphs. 16 Original Passages ... Fantasia and Diana DeGarmo were the last two finalists, and Fantasia was crowned as the winner. The third season was won by Fantasia Barrino, who defeated Diana DeGarmo... ... Query Which season was the runner up of the third season of American Idol on? Query-Related Triples ... <American Idol (season 3), winner, Fantasia Barrino> , <American Idol (season 3), runner-up, Kree Harrison> , <Kree Harrison, took runner-up spot, American Idol>, ... Refinement Actions <refinement>delete_edge(American Idol (season 3), runner-up, Kree Harrison) insert_edge(American Idol (season 3), runner-up, Diana DeGarmo) insert_edge(Diana DeGarmo, runner-up of, American Idol (season 3))</refinement> Table 6: The case of refinement for Incorrectness issue involving factual contradiction. D.3 Case 3: Redundancy We mainly focus on two types of redundancy issues: coreference resolution and disambiguation. And we will give some examples to show how DeepRefine can deal with these issues. Coreference resolution. As shown in Table 7, the original knowledge base contains coreference issue: According to the original passages, the girls phone number is extracted as an knowledge item in the query-related triples, and is referenced by both James. However, as the query is about whose phone number did James receive during the beach outing, even with some contextual information in the query-related triples, the models still cannot figure out who is the girl mentioned in the triple. DeepRefine can correctly resolve this issue by first locating the potentially problematic region, then replacing the girls phone number with Samanthas phone number and inserting new edge between James and Samanthas phone number. Query Whose phone number did James receive during the beach outing? Query-Related Triples ... <James,left,the girls phone number>, <James,announced,decision to move in together with Samantha>, <James,took his three dogs to,beach outing>, <James,received,congrats James! Thats awesome.>, <John,wishing,James great time>, ... Original Passages ... James: Yesterday took my three dogs to beach outing to have fun and bond with other dogkeepers. John: Cool! Surely you gained new experience from communicating with other dog lovers! James: Yes, we had fun and even met one beautiful girl. Im thinking of asking her out on date! She left me her phone number, think Ill call tomorrow. John: Wow! Thats cool, whats her name? Be sure to call her, everything will work out! James: She is Samantha. Ill definitely call her! John: Yoohoo! Hope you have wonderful time! ... Refinement Actions <refinement>replace_node(the girls phone number, ber)insert_edge(James, received, Samanthas phone number)</refinement> Samanthas phone numTable 7: The case of refinement for coreference resolution issue. Disambiguation. As shown in Table 8, the original knowledge base contains potential disambiguation issue: The person in the original document, Ray Taylor, is extracted exactly as the same name 17 string in the knowledge base. However, this simple extraction strategy may cause the ambiguity of the knowledge base, for example, there could be another person with the same name string in the knowledge base, which may introduce some structural noise to the related triples. DeepRefine can correctly resolve this issue by first locating the potentially problematic subgraph, then replacing Ray Taylor with Ray Taylor (1888-12-01 to 1952-02-15), which smartly handles the disambiguation issue by adding more specific information to the knowledge items in the query-related triples. In this way, if any new people with the same name appear, the knowledge base will not confuse them. Query Which film has the director died later, Modern Husbands or The Fighting Vigilantes? Original Passages ... Ray Taylor (1 December 1888 - 15 February 1952) was an American film director. He directed 159 films between 1926 and 1949. His debut was the 1926 film serial Fighting with Buffalo Bill. Ravina is an Indian actress who acted in Dhallywood movies. She acted in Praner Cheye Priyo which film is considered as turning point of the career of Riaz. ... Refinement Actions Bayón Her- <refinement>insert_edge(Modern Husbands, rera)insert_edge(The Fighting Vigilantes, directed-by, Ray Taylor)insert_edge(Modern Husbands, released-on, 1948-01-01)insert_edge(The Fighting Vigilantes, releasedon, Ray Taylor (1888-12-01 to 1952-0215))</refinement> Query-Related Triples ... <"Fighting with Buffalo Bill",was directed by,Ray Taylor>, <Modern Husbands,starring,Olinda Bozán>, <Ray Taylor,debut film,"Fighting with Buffalo Bill">, <Ray Taylor,directed,159 films>, <Ray Taylor,lived from,1888-12-01 to 1952-02-15>, <Ray Taylor,was a,American film director> ... 1947-11-15)replace_node(Ray Taylor, directed-by, Luis Table 8: The case of refinement for disambiguation issue.

sections.title: E Prompt Templates
sections.content: In this section, we will provide the prompt templates for the DeepRefine model. We demonstrate the prompt templates for each reasoning step in DeepRefine in Figure 6, Figure 7, and Figure 8. Prompt Template for Answerable Judgement. System: As an advanced judgement assistant, your task is to judge whether the given question is answerable based on the provided KG context. Evaluate whether the given question is answerable based on the provided KG context. Output your judgement in the following format: <judge>Yes</judge> or <judge>No</judge> **Important:** You must think carefully about the question and the KG context before making your judgement. And output your judgement result directly in the specified format. User: Question: {question} Knowledge Graph (KG) context: {triples_string} Output: < judge > Yes </ judge > or < judge >No </ judge > Figure 6: The prompt template for answerable judgement in DeepRefine. 18 Prompt Template for Error Abduction. System: As an advanced error abduction assistant, your task is to analyze the error reasons based on the given interaction history. Analyze the reasons of the unanswerable questions based on the given interaction history. Output your analysis in the following format: <abduction>...</abduction> **Important:** You must think carefully about the interaction history before making your analysis. And output your analysis result directly in the specified format. User: Interaction history: {interaction_history} Output: < abduction >... </ abduction > Figure 7: The prompt template for error abduction in DeepRefine. Prompt Template for Refinement Action Generation. System: As an advanced knowledge graph refinement assistant, your task is to generate series of actions to refine the given KG to make it more suitable for answering the given question. Based on the given KG and the analysed error reasons, refine the given KG to make it more easily for retrieval and answering the given question. You have the following three types of actions to conduct: - insert_edge(subject, relation, object): Insert new edge into the KG to complete the missing information. - delete_edge(subject, relation, object): Delete an edge from the KG to remove the redundant information or conflicting information. - replace_node(old_entity, new_entity): Replace an entity in the KG to correct the errors or deal with disambiguation. Output series of actions in the following format: <refinement>insert_edge("...", "...", "...")delete_edge("...", "...", "...")replace_node("...", "...")...</refinement> **Important:** You must think carefully about the given KG and the analysed error reasons before making your refinement. DO NOT DELETE ANY IRRELEVANT TRIPLES FROM THE ORIGINAL KG. TRY TO KEEP THE ORIGINAL KG AS MUCH AS POSSIBLE. And output your refinement result directly in the specified format. User: Original Text: {original_text} KG: {triples_string} Question: {question} Error reasons: {error_reasons} Output: < refinement > insert _edge ("..." , "..." , "...") delete _edge ("..." , "..." , "...") replace _node ("..." , "...") ... </ refinement > Figure 8: The prompt template for refinement action generation in DeepRefine.

sections.title: F Limitations
sections.content: In this work, we propose general LLM-based reasoning model for agent-compiled knowledge base refinement. Although DeepRefine has shown promising performance in the experiments, there are still some potential improvements that can be made to the model. For example, we only construct the training data from HotpotQA with easy filtering criteria, which makes the quality of the training data not high enough. Finding out higher quality and more diverse training data is promising direction for future work. And how to combine DeepRefine with robust systematic refinement framework to make the performance better and more stable is also underexplored.
